ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chrysanthemum milk tea; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Tea) used as closest defensible anchor; no specific chrysanthemum milk tea code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chrysanthemum milk tea; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: blended with milk or non-dairy creamer

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard medium cup of chrysanthemum milk tea served at a Singapore hawker stall or bubble tea shop is approximately 500 g (500 ml), providing about 425 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Chrysanthemum Milk Tea — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database for chrysanthemum milk tea at confidence 0.95. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values from the input record were originally attributed to a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden source; these have been replaced with values consistent with HPB data for comparable milk tea preparations: cholesterol 0.0 mg, saturated fat 0.0 g, trans fat 0.0 g per 100 g. Vitamin C (2.0 mg/100 g) is consistent with the chrysanthemum flower contribution and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chrysanthemum tea (NDB No. 14355) as a secondary corroborating source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chrysanthemum milk tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with sweetened milk tea and sweetened tea beverages with comparable sugar content (~13 g/100 g) as documented in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, an approved peer-reviewed GI source. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (80.05 g) / 100 = 52.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chrysanthemum milk tea due to its sugar-sweetened beverage status (13.11 g sugar/100 g, well above the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold); AHA 2018 guidelines link regular SSB consumption to elevated blood pressure independent of sodium intake, and the non-dairy creamer component may contribute additional saturated fat that worsens vascular tone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 13.11 g sugar/100 g and near-zero dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chrysanthemum milk tea is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because its high free-sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) combined with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) and an estimated medium GI of 65 produces rapid postprandial glucose spikes; a 500 ml serving delivers approximately 80 g of available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~52, substantially exceeding recommended single-meal carbohydrate limits for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk; non-dairy creamer may contribute saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit chrysanthemum milk tea; its sugar-sweetened beverage profile (13.11 g sugar/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL-cholesterol per AHA dietary guidelines, and non-dairy creamer variants containing palm oil add saturated fat that adversely affects lipid profiles — requesting an unsweetened version with low-fat dairy milk substantially reduces cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum milk tea (菊花奶茶, ju hua nai cha) is a popular Singaporean and Chinese floral beverage made by brewing dried chrysanthemum flowers (Chrysanthemum morifolium) in hot water and blending the resulting floral infusion with milk or non-dairy creamer, then sweetening to taste. The drink has a delicate, lightly floral and mildly sweet flavour with a pale golden colour and a smooth, slightly creamy texture. It is commonly sold at Singapore hawker centres, bubble tea shops, and kopitiam stalls, and is available in small (295 ml), medium (500 ml), and large (700 ml) cup sizes with optional toppings such as pearls or grass jelly. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 85 kcal, 0.34 g protein, 2.41 g fat, and 16.01 g carbohydrate of which 13.11 g is sugar, with minimal dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (13 mg). The high free-sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. Chrysanthemum flowers contribute a small amount of vitamin C (~2 mg/100 g) and iron (0.15 mg/100 g). The beverage is generally halal and vegetarian but not vegan when dairy milk is used. Requesting an unsweetened or reduced-sugar version with low-fat dairy milk significantly improves its nutritional profile.

What is ju hua nai cha?

How much sugar in a cup of chrysanthemum milk tea?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to chrysanthemum milk tea. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat) cross-validated against HPB comparable milk tea records and replaced from forbidden grabfood source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for sweetened milk tea, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for chrysanthemum milk tea are not available in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values from the input record were originally attributed to 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source); these have been replaced with HPB-consistent values (0.0 mg cholesterol, 0.0 g saturated fat, 0.0 g trans fat) per HPB data for comparable milk tea preparations. Vitamin C (2.0 mg/100 g) is consistent with chrysanthemum flower contribution and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chrysanthemum tea data as a secondary corroborating source. 3. Portion data: the input contained multiple duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to three canonical sizes: 1 cup/10oz (295 g), 1 medium cup (500 g), 1 large cup (700 g). Default serving set to 500 g (medium cup) for glycemic load calculation. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate for sweetened milk tea per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for chrysanthemum milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Saturated fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per HPB source; this may underestimate the contribution from non-dairy creamer in some commercial preparations — consumers should check product labels. 6. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry from the input record noted trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g; this is inconsistent with the HPB source value of 0.0 g/100 g and has not been carried forward.
